   "source": [
    "(learning)=\n",
    "# Deep Bayesian predictive coding"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",

  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 2,
   "id": "6e337fd3-5a3e-4e0f-ab4f-e055cebfb7ff",

   "source": [
    "import jax.numpy as jnp\n",
    "import matplotlib.animation as animation\n",
    "import matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n",
    "import numpy as np\n",
    "import seaborn as sns\n",
    "import treescope\n",
    "from IPython.display import HTML\n",
    "\n",
    "from pyhgf.model import DeepNetwork, Network\n",
    "from pyhgf.plots.graphviz.plot_network import plot_deep_network\n",
    "\n",
    "np.random.seed(123)\n",
    "plt.rcParams[\"figure.constrained_layout.use\"] = True\n",
    "\n",
    "treescope.basic_interactive_setup(autovisualize_arrays=True)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "d5341c9b-cbd3-4125-933f-52af82a18ee9",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "```{warning}\n",
    "The features exposed here are still a work in progress.\n",
    "```"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "94cee49c-4e25-48b9-9fe6-57e4a31fbf6b",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "The hierarchical Gaussian filter is built on top of a generative model that is governed by Gaussian random walks, which is best used to model the time-resolved evolution of beliefs in volatile environments. But the framework can easily extend to traditional applications of predictive coding, such as deep neural networks, where the variational message passing replaces the use of iterative gradient descent during inference.\n",
    "\n",
    "In this notebook, we show that the **prospective configuration** in predictive coding networks can be performed by one-shot variational updates, removing the need for gradient descent over the energy function, while learning expected precision in the hidden layers, which is often fixed in other approaches. We illustrate this with the \"bear example\" from {cite:p}`Song2024` and on a classification task with deep networks.\n",
    "\n",
    "```{figure} https://media.springernature.com/full/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41593-023-01514-1/MediaObjects/41593_2023_1514_Fig1_HTML.png?as=webp\n",
    "---\n",
    "name: learning-prospective-config-task\n",
    "---\n",
    "Learning with prospective configuration.\n",
    "\n",
    "```"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "f6c811a5",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "## Learning in deep networks\n",
    "\n",
    "### Prospective configuration"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "3a858473",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "In standard backpropagation, weights are updated using gradients computed from a fixed forward pass. The network's activations are \"frozen\" while the error signal propagates backward. **Prospective configuration** {cite:p}`Song2024` takes a different approach: before any weight change, the network first *infers* the most likely activations at every layer by settling prediction errors across the whole hierarchy. Only once this inference step has converged are the weights updated. This two-phase process (infer activations, then update weights) prevents the catastrophic interference that arises when a local weight change inadvertently distorts representations elsewhere in the network.\n",
    "\n",
    "In `pyhgf`, this is implemented naturally through the belief propagation cycle. At each observation:\n",
    "\n",
    "1. **Prediction**: predictors ($x$) are provided in the leaf nodes. Each node generates a top-down prediction for its children via a nonlinear coupling function $g(\\cdot)$.\n",
    "2. **Observation & prediction errors**: the observed values ($y$) are compared with predictions, and precision-weighted prediction errors propagate upward through the hierarchy.\n",
    "3. **Posterior update**: node activations (means and precisions) are updated to minimise free energy, settling the network into a new equilibrium. This is comparable to the *prospective configuration* step.\n",
    "4. **Weight update**: only after the activations have settled are the coupling strengths (weights) adjusted using the prediction errors and the inferred activations.\n",
    "\n",
    "We illustrate this using the \"bear\" example network from {cite:t}`Song2024`."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 3,
   "id": "96755731-c897-4776-b571-03bd76a8f533",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# here x represents the visual input (River / No River)\n",
    "x = np.array([1.0, 1.0] * 120)\n",
    "x += np.random.normal(size=x.shape) / 100\n",
    "\n",
    "# y represents the auditory and olfactory stimuli\n",
    "y = np.array([\n",
    "    x,\n",
    "    np.concat([\n",
    "        np.array([1.0, 1.0] * 40),\n",
    "        np.array([-1.0, -1.0] * 40),\n",
    "        np.array([1.0, 1.0] * 40),\n",
    "    ])\n",
    "    + np.random.normal(size=x.shape) / 100,\n",
    "]).T"
   ]
  },

   "source": [
    "# We start by defining a simple network with two branches\n",
    "network = (\n",
    "    Network(update_type=\"unbounded\")\n",
    "    .add_nodes(n_nodes=2, precision=2.0, expected_precision=2.0)\n",
    "    .add_nodes(\n",
    "        kind=\"volatile-state\",\n",
    "        value_children=[0, 1],\n",
    "        autoconnection_strength=0,\n",
    "        tonic_volatility=-4.0,\n",
    "        coupling_fn=(jnp.tanh, jnp.tanh),\n",
    "    )\n",
    "    .add_nodes(\n",
    "        value_children=2,\n",
    "        autoconnection_strength=0,\n",
    "        coupling_fn=(jnp.tanh,),\n",
    "        precision=2.0,\n",
    "        expected_precision=2.0,\n",
    "    )\n",
    ")\n",
    "network.plot_network()"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "d5dff455-f8d3-4b36-b36e-b6ce74fc6f5d",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "Deep networks trained for classification purposes differ from other predictive coding networks as both roots and leaves should receive inputs (predictors and outcomes, respectively)."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 5,
   "id": "40c554b5-b869-498d-bca9-0e62d27673f2",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "scrolled": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "network.fit(\n",
    "    x=x,\n",
    "    y=y,\n",
    "    inputs_x_idxs=(3,),\n",
    "    inputs_y_idxs=(0, 1),\n",
    "    learning_kind=\"dynamic\",\n",
    "    lr=1.0,\n",
    "    record_trajectories=True,\n",
    ");"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 6,
   "id": "5396f0c3-d38b-45e9-a12d-d75b468712e8",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },

    ]
   },

   ],
   "source": [
    "_, axs = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5), nrows=3, sharey=True, sharex=True)\n",
    "\n",
    "network.plot_nodes(2, show_surprise=False, axs=axs[0])\n",
    "network.plot_nodes(0, show_surprise=False, axs=axs[1])\n",
    "network.plot_nodes(1, show_surprise=False, axs=axs[2])\n",

  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "46ec8259-272c-4164-bd7c-3cf3da08b016",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "This example illustrates the effectiveness of the variational update to replace the prospective configuration step based on gradient descent. As new observations contradict the expected outcomes (i.e., observing the river without hearing the water, from trials 40 to 80 in the bottom panel), the network efficiently reorganizes without interfering with other predictions (i.e,. still expecting smelling the salmon while seeing the water, top panel, trials 40 to 80)."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "7bcd2bcc-ca33-4190-9dee-953b20ea90ed",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "```{hint} Volatile state nodes\n",
    "In a predictive coding network, two types of state nodes can be used to build the hierarchy. Continuous-state nodes are the standard HGF nodes: their precision is controlled by external volatility parents connected through dedicated volatility edges, and their mean persists across time steps by default (`autoconnection_strength`=1.0). This makes them well-suited for tracking slowly drifting quantities or serving as input/output layers that receive observations. Volatile-state nodes bundle a value level and an internal volatility level into a single node, removing the need for separate volatility parents. Their mean resets at each time step by default (`autoconnection_strength`=0.0), making them behave like stateless hidden units whose activation is determined entirely by incoming predictions. In practice, volatile-state nodes are the natural building block for hidden layers in deep predictive coding networks; they are more parameter-efficient than continuous-state nodes because the volatility coupling is handled internally, and their stateless default mirrors the feedforward activations of a conventional neural network.\n",
    "```"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "e95d1b33",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "### Weight update\n",
    "\n",
    "Once the network has settled into its new posterior (the prospective configuration step), the coupling strengths $w_i$ between a child node and its value parents are updated. Let $\\text{PE}$ denote the value prediction error at the child node, $\\pi_{\\text{child}}$ its posterior precision, and $g(\\mu_i)$ the activation of parent $i$ passed through the coupling function $g$.\n",
    "\n",
    "**Fixed learning rate.** With a constant step size $\\eta$:\n",
    "\n",
    "$$\n",
    "\\Delta w_i \\;=\\; \\eta \\;\\cdot\\; \\text{PE} \\;\\cdot\\; \\pi_{\\text{child}} \\;\\cdot\\; g(\\mu_i)\n",
    "$$\n",
    "\n",
    "**Dynamic (precision-weighted) learning rate.** When no fixed rate is specified, the update uses a Kalman-gain-like rule that automatically scales the step size by the relative precision of parent and child:\n",
    "\n",
    "$$\n",
    "K_i = \\frac{\\pi_{\\text{parent}_i}}{\\pi_{\\text{parent}_i} + \\pi_{\\text{child}}}\n",
    "$$\n",
    "\n",
    "$$\n",
    "\\Delta w_i \\;=\\; K_i \\;\\cdot\\; \\text{PE} \\;\\cdot\\; g(\\mu_i)\n",
    "$$\n",
    "\n",
    "In the dynamic case, precise parents exert a larger influence on the weight update while uncertain parents are updated more cautiously. This precision weighting is what produces the depth-dependent learning effect demonstrated in the next section."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "5f7e55d6-c41c-4de5-a3f4-91c740729b4b",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "### Input precision controls the depth of weight updates\n",
    "\n",
    "One natural consequence that emerges from this framework is that neural activations are defined by their precision, and as a result, the precision of the inputs (both predictors and outcomes) controls the strengths of prediction errors and the amplitude of weight updates in the vicinity of information flows. For example, more precise outcomes will guide weight updates to be larger at the nodes close to the inputs, and a more precise predictor will guide weight updates to be larger close to the internal representation.\n",
    "\n",
    "We can simulate this with a deep stack of hidden nodes whose predictions and outcomes differ, which forces the network to reorganize. Here, we show that the balance of precision between predictors and outcomes shapes the depth of weight updates."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 7,
   "id": "4d11d4ec-3b37-419f-946c-efe8520d717c",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "linear = lambda x: x"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 8,
   "id": "577ded2a-5c46-45ac-b692-7087be048bef",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "x = np.array([1.0] * 200)\n",
    "# x += np.random.normal(size=x.shape) / 100\n",
    "\n",
    "y = np.array([0.0] * 200)\n",
    "# y += np.random.normal(size=y.shape) / 100"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 9,
   "id": "5e96f8d8-4b64-43a9-a21b-5d42bbec00ae",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# We start by defining two networks with varying precision\n",
    "# at the predictor and outcome levels\n",
    "high_outcome_precision_network = (\n",
    "    Network(update_type=\"unbounded\")\n",
    "    .add_nodes(n_nodes=1, precision=1e2, expected_precision=1e2)\n",
    "    .add_nodes(\n",
    "        kind=\"volatile-state\",\n",
    "        value_children=0,\n",
    "        coupling_fn=(linear,),\n",
    "        tonic_volatility=-2.0,\n",
    "        autoconnection_strength=0,\n",
    "    )\n",
    "    .add_nodes(\n",
    "        kind=\"volatile-state\",\n",
    "        value_children=1,\n",
    "        coupling_fn=(linear,),\n",
    "        tonic_volatility=-2.0,\n",
    "        autoconnection_strength=0,\n",
    "    )\n",
    "    .add_nodes(\n",
    "        kind=\"volatile-state\",\n",
    "        value_children=2,\n",
    "        coupling_fn=(linear,),\n",
    "        tonic_volatility=-2.0,\n",
    "        autoconnection_strength=0,\n",
    "    )\n",
    "    .add_nodes(\n",
    "        kind=\"volatile-state\",\n",
    "        value_children=3,\n",
    "        coupling_fn=(linear,),\n",
    "        tonic_volatility=-2.0,\n",
    "        autoconnection_strength=0,\n",
    "    )\n",
    "    .add_nodes(\n",
    "        kind=\"volatile-state\",\n",
    "        value_children=4,\n",
    "        coupling_fn=(linear,),\n",
    "        tonic_volatility=-2.0,\n",
    "        autoconnection_strength=0,\n",
    "        precision=1.0,\n",
    "        expected_precision=1.0,\n",
    "    )\n",
    ")\n",
    "\n",
    "high_predictor_precision_network = (\n",
    "    Network(update_type=\"unbounded\")\n",
    "    .add_nodes(n_nodes=1, precision=1.0, expected_precision=1.0)\n",
    "    .add_nodes(\n",
    "        kind=\"volatile-state\",\n",
    "        value_children=0,\n",
    "        coupling_fn=(linear,),\n",
    "        tonic_volatility=-2.0,\n",
    "        autoconnection_strength=0,\n",
    "    )\n",
    "    .add_nodes(\n",
    "        kind=\"volatile-state\",\n",
    "        value_children=1,\n",
    "        coupling_fn=(linear,),\n",
    "        tonic_volatility=-2.0,\n",
    "        autoconnection_strength=0,\n",
    "    )\n",
    "    .add_nodes(\n",
    "        kind=\"volatile-state\",\n",
    "        value_children=2,\n",
    "        coupling_fn=(linear,),\n",
    "        tonic_volatility=-2.0,\n",
    "        autoconnection_strength=0,\n",
    "    )\n",
    "    .add_nodes(\n",
    "        kind=\"volatile-state\",\n",
    "        value_children=3,\n",
    "        coupling_fn=(linear,),\n",
    "        tonic_volatility=-2.0,\n",
    "        autoconnection_strength=0,\n",
    "    )\n",
    "    .add_nodes(\n",
    "        kind=\"volatile-state\",\n",
    "        value_children=4,\n",
    "        coupling_fn=(linear,),\n",
    "        tonic_volatility=-2.0,\n",
    "        autoconnection_strength=0,\n",
    "        precision=1e2,\n",
    "        expected_precision=1e2,\n",
    "    )\n",
    ")"
   ]
  },

    "high_predictor_precision_network.fit(\n",
    "    x=x,\n",
    "    y=y,\n",
    "    inputs_x_idxs=(5,),\n",
    "    inputs_y_idxs=(0,),\n",
    "    learning_kind=\"dynamic\",\n",
    "    lr=1.0,\n",
    "    record_trajectories=True,\n",
    ")\n",
    "high_outcome_precision_network.fit(\n",
    "    x=x,\n",
    "    y=y,\n",
    "    inputs_x_idxs=(5,),\n",
    "    inputs_y_idxs=(0,),\n",
    "    learning_kind=\"dynamic\",\n",
    "    lr=1.0,\n",
    "    record_trajectories=True,\n",
    ");"

   "source": [
    "_, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 6))\n",
    "\n",
    "# high prediction precision\n",
    "# -------------------------\n",
    "palette = sns.color_palette(\"mako\", 6)\n",
    "handles, labels = [], []\n",
    "for color, time_step in zip(palette, [4, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200]):\n",
    "    expected_means = [\n",
    "        high_predictor_precision_network.node_trajectories[i][\"expected_mean\"][\n",
    "            time_step\n",
    "        ]\n",
    "        for i in range(6)\n",
    "    ]\n",
    "    scatterplot = ax.scatter(\n",
    "        range(5, -1, -1),\n",
    "        expected_means,\n",
    "        s=150,\n",
    "        color=color,\n",
    "        label=time_step,\n",
    "        edgecolor=\"k\",\n",
    "    )\n",
    "    handles.append(scatterplot)\n",
    "    labels.append(f\"Step = {time_step}\")\n",
    "    ax.plot(range(5, -1, -1), expected_means, color=color, zorder=-1)\n",
    "\n",
    "first_legend = ax.legend(\n",
    "    handles=handles, labels=labels, title=\"Precise predictor\", bbox_to_anchor=(1.3, 1.1)\n",
    ")\n",
    "ax.add_artist(first_legend)\n",
    "\n",
    "# high outcome precision\n",
    "# ----------------------\n",
    "palette = sns.color_palette(\"rocket\", 6)\n",
    "handles, labels = [], []\n",
    "for color, time_step in zip(palette, [4, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200]):\n",
    "    expected_means = [\n",
    "        high_outcome_precision_network.node_trajectories[i][\"expected_mean\"][time_step]\n",
    "        for i in range(6)\n",
    "    ]\n",
    "    scatterplot = ax.scatter(\n",
    "        range(5, -1, -1),\n",
    "        expected_means,\n",
    "        s=150,\n",
    "        color=color,\n",
    "        label=time_step,\n",
    "        edgecolor=\"k\",\n",
    "    )\n",
    "    handles.append(scatterplot)\n",
    "    labels.append(f\"Step = {time_step}\")\n",
    "    ax.plot(range(5, -1, -1), expected_means, color=color, zorder=-1)\n",
    "second_legend = ax.legend(\n",
    "    handles=handles, labels=labels, title=\"Precise outcome\", bbox_to_anchor=(1.3, 0.5)\n",
    ")\n",
    "\n",
    "plt.xticks(\n",
    "    ticks=[0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5],\n",
    "    labels=[\"Predictor\", \"Node 4\", \"Node 3\", \"Node 2\", \"Node 1\", \"Outcome\"],\n",
    ")\n",
    "ax.set(\n",
    "    ylabel=\"Activation level\",\n",
    "    title=\"Predictor and outcome precisions \\n balance the depth of weight updates\",\n",
    ")\n",
    "ax.grid(linestyle=\"--\")\n",
    "\n",
    "sns.despine()"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "2c355a01-b1aa-4c2a-b838-fcc15fdd8b53",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "## Building Deep-Network Structures in pyhgf\n",
    "\n",
    "### Backends\n",
    "\n",
    "As of version `0.2.9`, **PyHGF** supports the creation of deep neural networks for classification tasks using:\n",
    "\n",
    "- {py:class}`pyhgf.model.Network`: using the standard network class. Deep networks have to be built manually using {py:class}`pyhgf.model.Network.add_nodes`. This approach is more flexible, but will rapidly struggle with large structures (> 20 nodes) as each update function is cached by JAX.\n",
    "- {py:class}`pyhgf.rshgf.Network`: is the Rust equivalent and will scale efficiently to larger structures, while remaining flexible in the network configuration. It supports layered designs with {py:meth}`pyhgf.rshgf.Network.add_layer`. It is not differentiable.\n",
    "- {py:class}`pyhgf.model.DeepNetwork`: is a vectorised JAX implementation, differentiable, and the fastest solution.\n",
    "\n",
    "Here, we demonstrate two new high-level functions for constructing layered, fully connected value-parent structures in `pyhgf`:\n",
    "\n",
    "- **`add_layer()`** – adds a *single* fully connected parent layer.\n",
    "- **`add_layer_stack()`** – builds *multiple* layers at once, similar to `Sequential` in deep-learning frameworks.\n",
    "\n",
    "These functions allow HGF models to be composed in a deep-network style while remaining fully compatible with the probabilistic belief-update dynamics."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "a58260d9-b4c1-45c5-a64d-fb315d555070",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "### Adding fully connected Layers with `add_layer`\n",
    "\n",
    "`add_layer` provides fine-grained control, letting you manually construct each layer.\n",
    "\n",
    "This is useful when each layer should have different hyperparameters (precision, tonic volatility, autoconnection strength, etc.). The function creates a fully connected parent layer, in which each parent node connects to all children below it.\n",
    "\n",
    "By default, `add_layer` automatically connects to all orphan nodes (nodes without value parents). You can also specify `value_children` explicitly to control which nodes the layer connects to."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 13,
   "id": "66f59511-ffe5-4f46-897f-895b06e5d7ed",

     "execution_count": 13,
     "metadata": {},
     "output_type": "execute_result"
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "# Or chain them in a single expression (like Keras/PyTorch):\n",
    "net = (\n",
    "    DeepNetwork(coupling_fn=jnp.tanh)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=2)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=5, tonic_volatility=-1.0)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=3, tonic_volatility=-2.0)\n",
    ")\n",
    "\n",
    "# Visualize the network structure\n",
    "plot_deep_network(net)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "d6b67ab6-9b4b-4cf2-b92e-0fd668a0a658",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "### Adding multiple layers with `add_layer_stack`\n",
    "\n",
    "`add_layer_stack` provides a compact way to build several fully connected parent layers at once. Instead of adding each layer manually, you simply specify the desired layer sizes (e.g., [3, 16, 32]), and the function creates them sequentially. Each layer is fully connected to the one below, using the same hyperparameters for all layers you add (precision, tonic volatility, autoconnection strength, etc.).\n",
    "\n",
    "This is ideal when you want to quickly prototype deep hierarchical networks or mimic the \"stacked layer\" construction found in deep learning frameworks.\n",
    "\n",
    "Like `add_layer`, it also supports method chaining and auto-connects to orphan nodes by default."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 14,
   "id": "6406ed57-7932-416a-bcf8-eef93ec87d01",
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [
    {

      "image/svg+xml": [

   ],
   "source": [
    "# Add 3 fully connected parent layers (4→4→16→32) using method chaining\n",
    "net = (\n",
    "    DeepNetwork()\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=4)\n",
    "    .add_layer_stack(\n",
    "        layer_sizes=[4, 16, 32],\n",
    "        tonic_volatility=-1.0,\n",
    "    )\n",
    ")\n",
    "\n",
    "plot_deep_network(net)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "de2a07cc-dbe7-4489-abda-92c3cefc1962",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "Deep networks often require weights to be initialised using strategies that conserve the balance of variance in the inputs and output nodes. The classes have a {py:meth}`pyhgf.DeepNetwork.weight_initialisation` method that supports the most popular strategies."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 15,
   "id": "c6010839-07b6-41fb-a737-d4ca74d63326",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "net.weight_initialisation(strategy=\"xavier\");"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "4e98794d",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "## Binary classification on a two-moons dataset\n",
    "\n",
    "We now demonstrate `DeepNetwork` on a non-linearly separable problem: the classic *two moons* dataset. We train a **2 → 16 → 16 → 16 → 16 → 16 → 16 → 16 → 16 → 1 (binary)** predictive coding network with `tanh` coupling functions and a binary output layer, using the Adam optimiser. After training, we visualise how the learned decision boundary evolves across epochs."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "2e210752",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "### Generate the dataset\n",
    "\n",
    "We create a synthetic two-moons dataset with 500 samples and split it into 80% training / 20% test. We also pre-compute the mesh grid that will be used for the decision boundary heatmap."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 16,
   "id": "1c62a323",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "outputs": [
    {
     "name": "stdout",
     "output_type": "stream",
     "text": [
      "Training: 800 samples  |  Test: 200 samples\n",
      "Grid points: 37,848\n"
     ]
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "# --- Two-moons dataset ---\n",
    "def make_moons(n_samples=500, noise=0.15, seed=42):\n",
    "    \"\"\"Generate a two-moons dataset.\"\"\"\n",
    "    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)\n",
    "    n_half = n_samples // 2\n",
    "    theta_upper = np.linspace(0, np.pi, n_half)\n",
    "    x_upper = np.column_stack([np.cos(theta_upper), np.sin(theta_upper)])\n",
    "    theta_lower = np.linspace(0, np.pi, n_samples - n_half)\n",
    "    x_lower = np.column_stack([1 - np.cos(theta_lower), 1 - np.sin(theta_lower) - 0.5])\n",
    "    X = np.vstack([x_upper, x_lower]) + rng.normal(scale=noise, size=(n_samples, 2))\n",
    "    y = np.hstack([np.zeros(n_half), np.ones(n_samples - n_half)])\n",
    "    idx = rng.permutation(n_samples)\n",
    "    return X[idx].astype(np.float32), y[idx].astype(np.int32)\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "N_SAMPLES = 1000\n",
    "X_moons, y_moons = make_moons(n_samples=N_SAMPLES, noise=0.15, seed=42)\n",
    "\n",
    "# Train / test split (80 / 20)\n",
    "n_train = int(0.8 * N_SAMPLES)\n",
    "X_train_m, X_test_m = X_moons[:n_train], X_moons[n_train:]\n",
    "y_train_m, y_test_m = y_moons[:n_train], y_moons[n_train:]\n",
    "\n",
    "# Mesh grid for decision boundary\n",
    "h = 0.02\n",
    "x_min, x_max = X_moons[:, 0].min() - 0.5, X_moons[:, 0].max() + 0.5\n",
    "y_min, y_max = X_moons[:, 1].min() - 0.5, X_moons[:, 1].max() + 0.5\n",
    "xx, yy = np.meshgrid(np.arange(x_min, x_max, h), np.arange(y_min, y_max, h))\n",
    "grid = jnp.array(np.column_stack([xx.ravel(), yy.ravel()]), dtype=jnp.float32)\n",
    "\n",
    "print(f\"Training: {X_train_m.shape[0]} samples  |  Test: {X_test_m.shape[0]} samples\")\n",
    "print(f\"Grid points: {grid.shape[0]:,}\")"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 17,
   "id": "9b5e29ce",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "outputs": [
    {
     "data": {
      "image/png": "",
      "text/plain": [
       "<Figure size 500x500 with 1 Axes>"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {},
     "output_type": "display_data"
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "# Quick scatter of the raw data\n",
    "fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 5))\n",
    "for cls, color in [(0, \"#3b4cc0\"), (1, \"#b40426\")]:\n",
    "    mask = y_moons == cls\n",
    "    ax.scatter(\n",
    "        X_moons[mask, 0],\n",
    "        X_moons[mask, 1],\n",
    "        marker=\"o\",\n",
    "        s=20,\n",
    "        edgecolors=\"k\",\n",
    "        linewidths=0.5,\n",
    "        c=color,\n",
    "        label=f\"Class {cls}\",\n",
    "    )\n",
    "ax.set(xlabel=\"$x_1$\", ylabel=\"$x_2$\", title=\"Two-Moons Dataset\")\n",
    "ax.legend()\n",
    "plt.minorticks_on()\n",
    "sns.despine()"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "8b9b16e4",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "### Build the network\n",
    "\n",
    "We construct a **2 → 16 → 16 → 16 → 16 → 16 → 16 → 16 → 16 → 1 (binary)** `DeepNetwork` with `tanh` coupling functions and He weight initialisation. The binary output layer applies a sigmoid internally, producing probabilities directly."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 18,
   "id": "16f5c419",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "outputs": [
    {
     "name": "stdout",
     "output_type": "stream",
     "text": [
      "Architecture: 2 → 16 → 16 → 1 (binary)\n",
      "Layers: 14  |  Nodes: 195\n"
     ]
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "# Build: 2 → 16 → 16 → 1 (binary output)\n",
    "clf_net = (\n",
    "    DeepNetwork(coupling_fn=jnp.tanh)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=1, kind=\"binary\")\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=16, tonic_volatility=-4.0, tonic_volatility_vol=-2.0)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=16, tonic_volatility=-4.0, tonic_volatility_vol=-2.0)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=16, tonic_volatility=-4.0, tonic_volatility_vol=-2.0)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=16, tonic_volatility=-4.0, tonic_volatility_vol=-2.0)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=16, tonic_volatility=-4.0, tonic_volatility_vol=-2.0)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=16, tonic_volatility=-4.0, tonic_volatility_vol=-2.0)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=16, tonic_volatility=-4.0, tonic_volatility_vol=-2.0)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=16, tonic_volatility=-4.0, tonic_volatility_vol=-2.0)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=16, tonic_volatility=-4.0, tonic_volatility_vol=-2.0)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=16, tonic_volatility=-4.0, tonic_volatility_vol=-2.0)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=16, tonic_volatility=-4.0, tonic_volatility_vol=-2.0)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=16, tonic_volatility=-4.0, tonic_volatility_vol=-2.0)\n",
    "    .add_layer(size=2, add_constant_input=False, coupling_fn=lambda x: x)\n",
    "    .weight_initialisation(\"he\", seed=0)\n",
    ")\n",
    "\n",
    "print(f\"Architecture: 2 → 16 → 16 → 1 (binary)\")\n",
    "print(f\"Layers: {clf_net.n_layers}  |  Nodes: {clf_net.n_nodes}\")"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "3009e4e8",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "### Train over multiple epochs\n",
    "\n",
    "We train for 100 epochs using the Adam optimiser and snapshot the decision boundary at regular intervals."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 19,
   "id": "75261a0d",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "outputs": [
    {
     "name": "stdout",
     "output_type": "stream",
     "text": [
      "Epoch   0 | train loss=0.3006, acc=0.886 | test  loss=0.5155, acc=0.835\n",
      "Epoch  10 | train loss=0.1488, acc=0.978 | test  loss=0.1389, acc=0.975\n",
      "Epoch  20 | train loss=0.0688, acc=0.975 | test  loss=0.0694, acc=0.975\n",
      "Epoch  30 | train loss=0.0550, acc=0.983 | test  loss=0.0442, acc=0.980\n",
      "Epoch  40 | train loss=0.0499, acc=0.986 | test  loss=0.0513, acc=0.985\n",
      "Epoch  50 | train loss=0.0350, acc=0.993 | test  loss=0.0222, acc=0.990\n",
      "Epoch  60 | train loss=0.0362, acc=0.986 | test  loss=0.0345, acc=0.985\n",
      "Epoch  70 | train loss=0.0341, acc=0.989 | test  loss=0.0374, acc=0.985\n",
      "Epoch  80 | train loss=0.0334, acc=0.989 | test  loss=0.0458, acc=0.980\n",
      "Epoch  90 | train loss=0.0345, acc=0.990 | test  loss=0.0510, acc=0.980\n",
      "Epoch 100 | train loss=0.0401, acc=0.980 | test  loss=0.0327, acc=0.985\n",
      "Epoch 110 | train loss=0.0306, acc=0.986 | test  loss=0.0380, acc=0.985\n",
      "Epoch 120 | train loss=0.0332, acc=0.989 | test  loss=0.0442, acc=0.990\n",
      "Epoch 130 | train loss=0.0250, acc=0.990 | test  loss=0.0340, acc=0.990\n",
      "Epoch 140 | train loss=0.0232, acc=0.989 | test  loss=0.0396, acc=0.990\n",
      "Epoch 150 | train loss=0.0237, acc=0.989 | test  loss=0.0431, acc=0.980\n",
      "Epoch 160 | train loss=0.0235, acc=0.990 | test  loss=0.0386, acc=0.990\n",
      "Epoch 170 | train loss=0.0257, acc=0.990 | test  loss=0.0499, acc=0.985\n",
      "Epoch 180 | train loss=0.0255, acc=0.993 | test  loss=0.0409, acc=0.985\n",
      "Epoch 190 | train loss=0.0224, acc=0.991 | test  loss=0.0308, acc=0.990\n",
      "Epoch 200 | train loss=0.0232, acc=0.990 | test  loss=0.0433, acc=0.985\n",
      "Epoch 210 | train loss=0.0228, acc=0.990 | test  loss=0.0428, acc=0.980\n",
      "Epoch 220 | train loss=0.0225, acc=0.990 | test  loss=0.0485, acc=0.985\n",
      "Epoch 230 | train loss=0.0262, acc=0.990 | test  loss=0.0537, acc=0.980\n",
      "Epoch 240 | train loss=0.0231, acc=0.990 | test  loss=0.0533, acc=0.980\n",
      "Epoch 250 | train loss=0.0157, acc=0.994 | test  loss=0.0637, acc=0.975\n",
      "Epoch 260 | train loss=0.0210, acc=0.989 | test  loss=0.0422, acc=0.985\n",
      "Epoch 270 | train loss=0.0438, acc=0.986 | test  loss=0.0376, acc=0.985\n",
      "Epoch 280 | train loss=0.0229, acc=0.990 | test  loss=0.0411, acc=0.980\n",
      "Epoch 290 | train loss=0.0216, acc=0.989 | test  loss=0.0379, acc=0.980\n",
      "Epoch 299 | train loss=0.0320, acc=0.984 | test  loss=0.0377, acc=0.985\n",
      "\n",
      "Saved 151 decision boundary snapshots\n"
     ]
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "NUM_EPOCHS = 300\n",
    "SNAPSHOT_EVERY = 2\n",
    "LR = 0.2\n",
    "\n",
    "# Prepare JAX arrays\n",
    "jax_X_train = jnp.array(X_train_m)\n",
    "jax_y_train = jnp.array(y_train_m, dtype=jnp.float32).reshape(-1, 1)\n",
    "jax_X_test = jnp.array(X_test_m)\n",
    "jax_y_test = jnp.array(y_test_m, dtype=jnp.float32).reshape(-1, 1)\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "def bce(probs, labels, eps=1e-7):\n",
    "    \"\"\"Binary cross-entropy from probabilities.\"\"\"\n",
    "    p = np.clip(probs, eps, 1 - eps)\n",
    "    return -np.mean(labels * np.log(p) + (1 - labels) * np.log(1 - p))\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "train_losses, test_losses = [], []\n",
    "train_accs, test_accs = [], []\n",
    "snapshots = {}  # epoch → probability grid\n",
    "\n",
    "for epoch in range(NUM_EPOCHS):\n",
    "    # Evaluate on test set (forward pass, no weight updates)\n",
    "    test_preds = np.array(clf_net.predict(jax_X_test)).ravel()\n",
    "    test_labels = np.array(y_test_m)\n",
    "    test_losses.append(bce(test_preds, test_labels))\n",
    "    test_accs.append(np.mean((test_preds > 0.5).astype(int) == test_labels))\n",
    "\n",
    "    # Train: one full pass through the training set\n",
    "    clf_net.fit(\n",
    "        jax_X_train, \n",
    "        jax_y_train, \n",
    "        lr=\"adam\", \n",
    "        learning_kind=\"dynamic\"\n",
    "    )\n",
    "\n",
    "    # Training metrics from the predictions during this epoch\n",
    "    train_preds = np.array(clf_net.predictions).ravel()\n",
    "    train_labels = np.array(y_train_m)\n",
    "    train_losses.append(bce(train_preds, train_labels))\n",
    "    train_accs.append(np.mean((train_preds > 0.5).astype(int) == train_labels))\n",
    "\n",
    "    # Snapshot decision boundary\n",
    "    if epoch % SNAPSHOT_EVERY == 0 or epoch == NUM_EPOCHS - 1:\n",
    "        snap_preds = np.array(clf_net.predict(grid)).ravel()\n",
    "        snapshots[epoch] = snap_preds.reshape(xx.shape)\n",
    "\n",
    "    if epoch % 10 == 0 or epoch == NUM_EPOCHS - 1:\n",
    "        print(\n",
    "            f\"Epoch {epoch:>3d} | \"\n",
    "            f\"train loss={train_losses[-1]:.4f}, acc={train_accs[-1]:.3f} | \"\n",
    "            f\"test  loss={test_losses[-1]:.4f}, acc={test_accs[-1]:.3f}\"\n",
    "        )\n",
    "\n",
    "print(f\"\\nSaved {len(snapshots)} decision boundary snapshots\")"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "59865bd6",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "### Training curves"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 20,
   "id": "66e40ef4",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "outputs": [
    {
     "data": {
      "image/png": "",
      "text/plain": [
       "<Figure size 1200x400 with 2 Axes>"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {},
     "output_type": "display_data"
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "fig, axs = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))\n",
    "\n",
    "axs[0].plot(train_losses, label=\"Train\", alpha=0.8, color=\"#4c72b0\")\n",
    "axs[0].plot(test_losses, label=\"Test\", alpha=0.8, color=\"#55a868\")\n",
    "axs[0].set(xlabel=\"Epoch\", ylabel=\"Binary Cross-Entropy\", title=\"Loss\")\n",
    "axs[0].legend()\n",
    "axs[0].grid(linestyle=\"--\")\n",
    "\n",
    "axs[1].plot(train_accs, label=\"Train\", alpha=0.8, color=\"#4c72b0\")\n",
    "axs[1].plot(test_accs, label=\"Test\", alpha=0.8, color=\"#55a868\")\n",
    "axs[1].set(xlabel=\"Epoch\", ylabel=\"Accuracy\", title=\"Accuracy\")\n",
    "axs[1].legend()\n",
    "axs[1].grid(linestyle=\"--\")\n",
    "\n",
    "sns.despine()"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "37a1b9e4",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "### Decision boundary evolution\n",
    "\n",
    "We animate the decision boundary heatmap across training epochs alongside the loss curve."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "b89f4dfc-f6a6-4f6d-a424-7651fd903db6",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "```{figure} ../images/two_moons_training.gif\n",
    "---\n",
    "name: two-moons-animation\n",
    "---\n",
    "The GIF shows how the predictive coding network gradually carves out a non-linear separator to distinguish the two moons.\n",
    "```"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "id": "bef1b5c5-6166-4d54-83b7-90b089e1fc45",
   "metadata": {
    "editable": true,
    "slideshow": {
     "slide_type": ""
    },
    "tags": []
   },
   "source": [
    "::::{note} Code to create this animation 👈\n",
    ":class: dropdown\n",
    "\n",
    ":::{code} python\n",
    "\n",
    "snapshot_epochs = sorted(snapshots.keys())\n",
    "\n",
    "fig, ax_boundary = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))\n",
    "\n",
    "# --- Decision boundary (initial frame) ---\n",
    "extent = [xx.min(), xx.max(), yy.min(), yy.max()]\n",
    "im = ax_boundary.imshow(\n",
    "    snapshots[snapshot_epochs[0]],\n",
    "    extent=extent, origin=\"lower\", aspect=\"auto\",\n",
    "    cmap=\"coolwarm\", vmin=0, vmax=1, alpha=0.8, interpolation=\"bilinear\",\n",
    ")\n",
    "for cls, marker, color in [(0, \"o\", \"#3b4cc0\"), (1, \"s\", \"#b40426\")]:\n",
    "    mask = y_moons == cls\n",
    "    ax_boundary.scatter(\n",
    "        X_moons[mask, 0], X_moons[mask, 1],\n",
    "        marker=marker, s=15, edgecolors=\"k\", linewidths=0.5,\n",
    "        c=color, label=f\"Class {cls}\", alpha=0.8, zorder=2,\n",
    "    )\n",
    "ax_boundary.set(xlabel=\"$x_1$\", ylabel=\"$x_2$\")\n",
    "ax_boundary.legend(loc=\"upper right\")\n",
    "ax_boundary.set_title(f\"Decision Boundary — epoch {snapshot_epochs[0]}\")\n",
    "sns.despine(fig=fig)\n",
    "plt.tight_layout()\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "def update(frame_idx):\n",
    "    epoch = snapshot_epochs[frame_idx]\n",
    "    im.set_data(snapshots[epoch])\n",
    "    ax_boundary.set_title(f\"Decision Boundary — epoch {epoch}\")\n",
    "    return [im]\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "anim = animation.FuncAnimation(\n",
    "    fig, update, frames=len(snapshot_epochs), interval=150, blit=False,\n",
    ")\n",
    "anim.save(\"two_moons_training.gif\", writer=\"pillow\", dpi=120)\n",
    "plt.close(fig)\n",
    "\n",
    "print(\"GIF saved to two_moons_training.gif\")\n",
    "HTML(anim.to_jshtml())\n",
    "\n",
    ":::\n",
    "\n",
    "::::"
   ]
  },
  {

   "id": "ef848ca4-3492-4b45-81d9-14e4133f23ae",
